name: nungal (prison goddess)
shortDescription: Nungal is the Sumerian goddess of prisons and the underworld, associated with the Ekur prison. She is considered a child of Ereshkigal and plays a role in the administration of justice and punishment in the afterlife.
description: Nungal is a Sumerian goddess primarily associated with prisons and the underworld. Her name translates to 'Great Princess'. She is believed to be a child of Ereshkigal, the Queen of the Underworld (Kur), and Gugalanna, Ereshkigal's first husband. Nungal's role involves overseeing the Ekur prison, a specific location within the Sumerian underworld where the wicked are punished after death. While myths directly detailing Nungal's actions are scarce, her existence highlights the Sumerian belief in a structured afterlife with consequences for one's actions in life. Her association with Ereshkigal positions her within the complex hierarchy of the Sumerian pantheon and its governance of death and justice. The Ekur prison, under Nungal's watchful eye, reflects the Sumerian understanding of morality and the repercussions of transgressions. The goddess likely played a significant role in the religious beliefs surrounding death, justice, and the fate of the soul after death, particularly in reinforcing societal norms and ethical behavior through the concept of post-mortem punishment. While not as prominently featured as other Sumerian deities like Inanna or Enki, Nungal provides valuable insight into the Sumerian worldview and their understanding of the afterlife as a place of accountability.

sources.title: The Electronic Text Corpus of Sumerian Literature
sources.author: University of Oxford

sources.title: Myths of the Underworld: A Guide to the Realms of the Dead
sources.author: Constance Classen
